high_value_elements: Correctly identifies full mu agonists (morphine, fentanyl, hydromorphone, methadone) and distinguishes from partial agonist (buprenorphine) and kappa agonist (butorphanol)
Correctly states NSAIDs work via COX enzyme inhibition reducing prostaglandin synthesis; distinguishes COX-1 vs COX-2 selectivity implications for GI safety
Correctly identifies grapiprant as an EP4 receptor antagonist, not a COX inhibitor; appropriate for dogs with OA including those with GI sensitivity

Correctly identifies ketamine (IV CRI, approximately 60 mg/L at 5-10 mL/kg/hr) and amantadine (3-5 mg/kg once daily orally) as the two primary NMDA antagonist options taught, with different routes and clinical contexts

Correctly identifies tramadol: M1 (O-desmethyltramadol) as the active mu-opioid metabolite; CYP2D6 required for conversion; many dogs have limited CYP2D6 activity making tramadol act primarily as an SNRI in dogs; cats have effective analgesic response at 2 mg/kg TID
Correctly identifies anti-NGF monoclonal antibodies: frunevetmab (Solensia) for cats, bedinvetmab (Librela) for dogs; mechanism is NGF blockade preventing TrkA receptor activation on nociceptors
Notes cats' glucuronidation deficiency (Phase II hepatic metabolism) as the basis for heightened caution with multiple analgesic drug classes in felines
States acetaminophen is absolutely contraindicated in cats due to lack of glucuronidation causing methemoglobinemia even at standard doses
major_concerns: Reverses partial and full agonist properties or cannot name specific drugs in each class
Cannot explain the feline NSAID caution beyond 'cats are sensitive to drugs'
Does not know tramadol's M1 metabolite or the species difference in CYP2D6 activity
Confuses corticosteroid and NSAID mechanism of action
Recommends acetaminophen for a cat

high_value_elements: Correctly avoids or restricts long-term NSAIDs in IRIS Stage 3-4 CKD cat (prostaglandin E2 required for afferent arteriolar tone; NSAID inhibition risks acute-on-chronic renal decompensation)
Includes Solensia (frunevetmab) anti-NGF as a preferred option for this CKD cat: renal-safe mechanism, monthly subcutaneous injection, specifically licensed for feline OA pain

Selects buprenorphine via oral transmucosal (OTM) route as the practical home opioid for cats; dose approximately 0.01-0.02 mg/kg q6-8h; notes ceiling effect on respiratory depression versus full mu agonists
Includes gabapentin with explicit dose reduction for Stage 3-4 CKD: half-life extends from approximately 4 hours in normal cats to 13-20 hours in Stage 3-4 CKD (linear correlation with IRIS stage)
Adds an NMDA antagonist component (amantadine 3-5 mg/kg once daily) to address the neuropathic pain flare component

Correctly administers zoledronate for osteosarcoma: 0.1-0.5 mg/kg IV over exactly 15 minutes in normal saline only (not dextrose), maximum 4 mg total; pre-screens for active dental disease (absolute contraindication due to jaw osteonecrosis risk)
major_concerns: Prescribes long-term NSAID in Stage 3 CKD without contraindication acknowledgment or renal monitoring plan
Does not recognize gabapentin half-life extension in CKD and prescribes standard dosing intervals
Selects a drug route impractical or unsafe for owner administration in cats
Fails to address the neuropathic pain component pharmacologically
Combines NSAID and corticosteroid without contraindication awareness
Administers zoledronate in dextrose solution or without checking for dental disease

high_value_elements: Immediately flags the NSAID plus corticosteroid combination as requiring intervention before dispensing (dual prostaglandin inhibition and mucosal barrier disruption causing GI hemorrhage risk)
Identifies bisphosphonate dental disease contraindication: active dental disease or planned dental procedures require bisphosphonate washout due to jaw osteonecrosis risk
Identifies boutique diet or DCM-associated breed risk as warranting cardiac screening before alpha-2 agonist use in certain patients
Correctly describes DEA Schedule II/III record-keeping requirements: drug log, biannual inventory, separate locked storage, disposal records
Prioritizes patient safety over prescribing convenience in all high-risk combinations
major_concerns: Does not recognize the NSAID plus corticosteroid combination as dangerous
Has no response to the concurrent prescription scenario and would dispense both without intervention
Cannot describe any controlled substance compliance requirement
Does not know the bisphosphonate dental disease contraindication

high_value_elements: Explains the GI hemorrhage mechanism of NSAID plus corticosteroid: NSAIDs reduce gastroprotective prostaglandins while corticosteroids reduce mucosal repair and increase acid secretion

Explains the renal hemodynamic mechanism of NSAID risk in CKD: afferent arteriolar tone is prostaglandin-dependent when renal perfusion pressure is compromised; NSAID blockade causes acute tubular injury
Justifies buprenorphine over full mu agonist for home use: ceiling effect on respiratory depression, practical OTM absorption in cats (~50% bioavailability via OTM), owner safety
Justifies Solensia as renal-safe: mechanism does not involve renal prostaglandin pathways; monthly injection reduces compliance failure
Justifies amantadine addition for neuropathic component: NMDA receptor wind-up is the mechanism underlying central sensitization in neuropathic pain; gabapentin alone does not address this receptor
major_concerns: Provides drug names without pharmacologic justification
Cannot explain the mechanism behind any contraindication
States conclusions without mechanism-based reasoning

high_value_elements: Acknowledges no NSAID is risk-free in CKD and articulates a clear monitoring plan if short-term use is considered: baseline creatinine, urine specific gravity, recheck at 7 days
Recognizes the evolving evidence base for anti-NGF monoclonal antibodies and acknowledges that long-term safety data beyond 2 years is still accumulating
Identifies opioid diversion as a real practice risk requiring proactive systemic safeguards
Acknowledges uncertainty about optimal gabapentin dosing in CKD cats and commits to starting low with clinical monitoring
Recognizes tramadol's limited evidence for analgesia in dogs and does not recommend it as a sole analgesic in that species
major_concerns: Presents drugs as safe in contexts where the fellowship explicitly identified risks
Cannot identify a single limitation or monitoring requirement for any drug selected
Ignores controlled substance compliance obligations entirely

high_value_elements: Discusses opioid-sparing rationale with specific protocol components: NSAID + gabapentinoid + NMDA antagonist + locoregional block for TPLO as a comprehensive opioid-minimizing construct

Integrates Epstein fellowship content on chronic pain medication updates: duloxetine approximately 1 mg/kg q24h orally for dogs for neuropathic/chronic pain; amitriptyline 0.5-2 mg/kg q12-24h dogs or 5-10 mg per cat once daily for feline interstitial cystitis and neuropathic pain

Distinguishes LDN (low-dose naltrexone) mechanism from opioid antagonism: at low doses, transient opioid receptor blockade triggers upregulation; anti-neuroinflammatory mechanism via glial TLR4 modulation

Discusses CBD evidence critically: starting dose 0.1-2 mg/kg BID; notes THC is toxic to pets (CNS depression, urinary leakage, tachycardia); requires product purity verification for THC content below 0.3%
major_concerns: Cannot construct a realistic opioid-free or opioid-sparing protocol for a specific surgical case
Discusses pharmacology only in abstract terms disconnected from specific doses and routes
Cannot distinguish the Epstein fellowship guidance on chronic pain drug selection from basic pharmacology concepts
